title: What Is the Minimum That We Call Life?

lines.text: Section two. What is the minimum that we call life?
Look around the natural world and life appears in extraordinary variety — elephants, redwoods, blue whales at one end, single-celled bacteria and yeasts at the other.
All of them share a few essential features.
Each has DNA encoding its biological programme.
Each has RNA and protein machinery executing that programme.
Each is enclosed by a lipid membrane that defines a compartment separating inside from outside — the basic structure we call a cell.
And the smallest of these — bacteria and other single-celled microbes — is the natural starting point for asking what the minimum requirements of life are.
Smaller than cells are viruses.
Viruses have DNA or RNA and proteins, and they replicate — but only by hijacking a host cell's machinery.
On their own, they do not metabolise, they do not reproduce, they do not maintain themselves.
By the strictest definitions, viruses are not life — they are aberrant genetic programmes that depend on real cells to perpetuate themselves.

Similar arguments apply to transposons — mobile DNA elements that copy themselves from one chromosomal location to another — and prions — misfolded proteins that propagate by inducing the same misfolding in other protein copies.
All of these are interesting biological phenomena, but they are not free-living, and free-living is one of the defining criteria of life.
So the core question becomes: what is the minimum free-living cell?
A growing body of research is converging on an answer through two complementary strategies.

Strategy one is to look across nature and find the smallest free-living organisms that already exist — these natural minimal cells set a kind of lower bound on what life can get away with under natural selection.

Strategy two is to take a natural cell with a small genome and systematically remove genes one by one, asking at each step whether the cell can still survive, until you have stripped down to the irreducible minimum.
Both strategies have surfaced surprising results, and the picture they paint together is the one the rest of this chapter develops.
It is worth noting at the outset that the minimum number of genes turns out to depend heavily on what conditions you require the cell to survive in.
A cell that needs to forage for amino acids in a hostile environment needs many more genes than a cell living in a nutrient-rich laboratory medium where every amino acid is provided.
So when we talk about "minimal life", we are really talking about minimal life under specific environmental conditions.
Keep that conditional in mind throughout.
